title: 第 13 節
en: component part of the Buddhist Canon, must be later than that of the earliest Brahmanic Sutras, because in the meantime the meaning of the word had been changed from short mnemonic sentences to fully developed discourses. Possibly Sutra was originally meant for the text to be elucidated in a sermon, so that the long Buddhistic sermons came to be called Suttas in consequence, Bnlmspati- S ut r as .

That some of the earlier philosophical Sutras were lost, is shown in the case of the Brihaspatif Sutras. These are said to have contained the doctrines of the out and out materialists, or sensualists, the Laukayatikas or TTarvakas, who deny the existence of everything beyond what is given by the senses. They are referred to by Bhaskaraiarya at Brahma-Sutras III, 3, 53 1, and as he gives an extract, it is likely that they still existed in his time, though no MS. of them has been found as yet in India. The same applies to such Sutras as the Vaikhanasa-Sutras, possibly intended for the Vanaprasthas, and the BhikshuSutras"1, quoted by Pawini, IV, 3, no, and intended, it would seem, for Brahmanic, and not yet for Buddhistic mendicants. It is a sad truth which we have to learn more and more, that of the old pre-Buddhistic literature we have but scanty fragments, and that even these may be, in some cases, mere reproductions of lost originals, as in the case of the Samkhya-Stitras. We know now that such Sutras could have been produced at any time, and we should not forget that even at present, in the general decay of Sanskrit scholarship, India still possesses scholars who can imitate Kalidasa, to say nothing of such poems as the Mahabharata and Eamaya^a, and so successfully that few scholars could tell the difference. It is not long ago that I received a Sanskrit treatise written in Sutras with a commentary, the work of a living scholar in India, which might have deceived many a European scholar of Sanskrit 1 Colebrooke, Misc. Essays a, I, 429.

• 3 They were identified by Taranatha Tarkavafcaspati with the VedantaSfttras ; see Siddhanta Kaunxudi, vol. i, p. 593, BOOKS OF REFERENCE. 87 literature 1. If that is possible now, if, as in the case of the Kapila-Sutras, it was possible in the fourteenth century, why should not the same have taken place during the period of the Kenaissance in India, nay even at an earlier time ? At all events, though grateful for what has been preserved, and preserved in what may seem to us an almost miraculous manner, we should not imagine that we possess all, or that we possess what we possess in its original form.
Books of Reference.

I shall mention here some of the most important works only, from which students of philosophy, particularly those ignorant of Sanskrit, may gain by themselves a knowledge of the six recognised systems of Indian Philosophy. The titles of the more important of the original Sanskrit texts may be found in Colebrooke's Miscellaneous Essays, vol. ii, p. 239 seq., and in the Catalogues, published since his time, of the various collections of Sanskrit MSS. in Europe and India.
For the Vedanta-philosophy of Badaraya^a the most useful book is Thibaut's English translation of the text of the Sutras and $amkara's commentary in the S. B* E., vols. xxxiv and xxxviii.
Of books written in German, Deussen's translation of the same work, 1887, preceded as it was by his 'System des Ved&nta/ 1883, can be thoroughly recommended.
Of the S&mkhya-system we have the Sutras translated by Ballantyne in 1882-1885, the Aphorisms of the S£mkhya Philosophy of Kapila, with illustrative extracts from the Commentaries, 1852, 1865, 1885.

In German we have the Samkhya-Prava/cana-Bh&shya, Vigwana-bhikshu's Commentar zu den Samkhya-Sutras, iibersetzt von E. Garbe, 1889. Also Aniruddha's Commentary and the original parts of Vedantin Mahadeva's commentary on the S&mkhya-Sutras, by Richard Garbe, 1 It is called Katantrafcfcftandafeprakriya by Zandrakanta Tarkalankara, 1896, and gives additional Sutras to the Katantra on Vedic Grammar. He makes no secret that Sutrara vnttis fcobhayam api mayaiva vyarafti, 4 the Sutra and the commentary, both were composed by me/ 88 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
Der Mondschein der Samkhya Wahrheit, Va/^aspatimisra's Samkhya-tattva-kaumudi, iibersetzt von R. Garbe, 1893, is also a very useful work.

The Samkhya Karika by f swarakrishna, translated from the Sanscrit by H. T. Colebrooke, also the Bhashya or commentary by Gaurapada; translated and illustrated by an original comment by H. H. Wilson, Oxford, 1837, may still be consulted with advantage.
Other useful works are : —
John Davies, Hindu Philosophy. The Sankhya Karika of Iswarakrishna, London, 1881.

Die Samkhya-Philosophie, nach den Quellen, von K. Garbe, Of the Purva-Mimamsa or simply Mimamsa, which deals chiefly with the nature and authority of the Veda with special reference to sacrificial and other duties, we have the Sutras with /Sabarasvamin's commentary published in the original; but there is as yet no book in English in which that system may be studied, except Professor Thibaut's translation of Laugakshi Bhaskara's Arthasamgraha, a short abstract of that philosophy, published in the Benares Sanskrit Series, No. 4.

The Vaiseshika system of philosophy may be studied in an English translation of its Sutras by A. E. Gough, Benares, 1873 ; also in a German translation by Roer, Zeitschrift der Deutschen Morgenlandischen Gesellschaft, vols. 21 and 22, and in some articles of mine in the same Journal of the German Oriental Society, 1849.

The Nyaya-Sutras of Gotama have been translated, with the exception of the last book, by Ballantyne, Allahabad, The Yoga-Sutras are accessible in an English translation by Rajendralala Mitra, in the Bibliotheca Indica, Nos. 462, 478, 482, 491, and 493.
Bates of the Philosophical Sfttras.

If we consider the state of philosophical thought in India such as it is represented to us in the Brahmawas and Upanishads, and afterwards in the canonical books of the Buddhists, we cannot wonder that all attempts at fixing DATES OP TH& PHILOSOPHICAL SUTRAS. 89 the dates of the six recognised systems of philosophy, nay even their mutual relationship, should hitherto have failed. It is true that Buddhism and (?ainism were likewise but two philosophical systems out of many, and that it has been possible to fix their dates. But if in their case we know something about their dates and their historical development, this is chiefly due to the social and political importance which they acquired during the fifth, the fourth, and the third centuries B. c., and not simply to their philosophical tenets. We know also that there were many teachers, contemporaries of Buddha, but they have left no traces in the literary history of India.

Nor should we forget that, though the date of the Buddhist Canon may be fixed, the date of many of the texts which we now possess and accept as canonical is by no means beyond the reach of doubt, In the Buddhist annals themselves other teachers such as (?tf&tiputra, the Nirgrantha, the founder of Gainism, PuraTia K&syapa, Kakuda K&tyayana, Ac/ita Kesakambali, Sam#aya Vairaj^i-putra, Gosali-putra, the Maskarin, are mentioned by the side of Gautama, the prince of the clan of the S&kyas. One of these only became known in history, 6r/z&tiputra, the Nirgrantha or gymnosophist, because the society founded by him, like the brotherhood founded by Buddha, developed into a powerful sect, the ffainas. Another, Gosali with the bamboo stick, originally an Agivaka, then a follower of Mahavira, became likewise the founder of a sect of his own, which, however, has now disappeared1. Cr/iatiputra or N&taputta was actually the senior of Buddha.

Though it seems likely that the founders of the six systems of philosophy, though not the authors of the Stitras which we possess, belonged to the same period of philosophical and religious fermentation which gave rise to the first spreading of Buddha's doctrines in India, it is by no means clear that any of these systems, in their literary form, are presupposed by Buddhism. This is owing to the vagueness of the quotations which are hardly ever given 1 Kern, Buddhismus, I, p. 182.

verbatim. In India, during the mnemonic period of literature, the contents of a book may have become considerably modified, while the title remained the same. Even at a much later time, when we see Bhartrihari (died 650 A.D.) referring to the Mimamsaka, S&mkhya, and Vaiseshika Darsanas, we have no right to conclude that he knew these Darsanas exactly as we know them, though he may well have known these philosophies after they had assumed their systematic form. Again, when he quotes Naiy&yikas, it by no means follows that he knew our Gotama-Sfttras, nor have we any right to say that our Gotama-Sutras existed in his time. It is possible, it is probable, but it is not certain. We must therefore be very careful not to rely too much on quotations from, or rather allusions to, other systems of philosophy.
S&wkliy a- Sutras.

The S&mkhya-Sutras, as we possess them, are very chary of references. They clearly refer to Vaiseshika and Nyaya, when they examine the six categories of the former (V, 85) and the sixteen Pad&rthas of the latter (V, 86). Whenever they refer to the ATIUS or atoms, we know that they have the Vaiseshika-philosophy in their minds; and once the Vaiseshikas are actually mentioned by name (I, 35). $ruti, which the Samkhyas were supposed to disregard, is very frequently appealed to, Smriti once (V, 123), and Vamadeva, whose name occurs in both Sruti and Smriti, is mentioned as one who had obtained spiritual freedom. But of individual philosophers we meet only with Saa^ndana AMrya (VI, 69^ and Pa>?&asikha (V, 32; VI,' 68), while the teachers, the Aiaryas, when mentioned in general, are explained as comprehending Kapila himself, as well as others.
Ved&nta-Stitras, The Ved&nta-Sutras contain more frequent references, but they too do not help us much for chronological purposes.

B&darftyaraa refers more or less clearly to the Buddhists, the ffainas, Pasupatas, and Patf&aratras, all of whom he is endeavouring to refute. He never refers, however, to any VEDANTA-STJTRAS. 9 1 literary work3 and even when he refers to other philosophical systems, he seems to avoid almost intentionally the recognised names of their authors, nay even their technical terms. Still it is clear that the systems of the PurvaMimamsa, the Yoga, Samkhya, and Vaiseshika were in his mind when he composed his Sutras, and among Mimamsic authorities he refers by name to ffaimini, Badari, AucMomi, Asmarathya, Kasakritsna, Karshnaguni, and Atreya, nay to a BadarayaTia also. We cannot be far wrong therefore if we assign the gradual formation of the six systems of philosophy to the period from Buddha (fifth century) to Asoka (third century), though we have to admit, particularly in the cases of Vedanta, S&mkhya, and Yoga a long previous development reaching back through Upanishads and Br&hma72,as to the very hymns of the Big-veda.

It is equally difficult to fix the relative position l of the great systems of philosophy,, because, as I explained before, they quote each other mutually. With regard to the relation of Buddhism to the six orthodox systems it seems to me that all we can honestly say is that schools of philosophy handing down doctrines very similar to those of our six classical or orthodox systems, are presupposed by the Buddhist Suttas. But this is very different from the opinion held by certain scholars that Buddha or his disciples actually borrowed from our Stitras. We know nothing of S&mkhya-literature before the S&mkhya-k&rikas, which belong to the sixth century after Christ. Even if we admit that the Tattva-samasa was an earlier work, how could we, without parallel dates, prove any actual borrowing on the part of Buddha or his disciples at that early time?

In the Upanishads and Br&hmaTias, though there is a common note running through them all, there is as yet great latitude and want of system, and a variety of opinions supported by different teachers and different schools. Even in the hymns we meet with great independence and individuality of thought, which occasionally seems to amount to downright scepticism and atheism.
1 Bhandarkar, Sawkhya Philosophy (1871), p. 3.

We must keep all this in mind if we wish to gain a correct idea of the historical1* origin and growth of what we are accustomed to call the six philosophical systems of India. We have seen already that philosophical discussions were not confined to the Brahmans, but that the Kshatriyas also took a very active and prominent part in the elaboration of such fundamental philosophical concepts as that of Atman or Self.

It is out of this floating mass of philosophical and religious opinion, which was common property in India, that the regular systems slowly emerged. Though we do not know in what form this took place, it is quite clear that what we now possess of philosophical manuals, in the form of Sutras, could not have been written down during the time when writing for any practical purposes except inscriptions on monuments and coins was still unknown in India, or at all events had not yet been employed for literary purposes, so far as we know.
zh: 佛教經藏的組成部分必定晚於最早的婆羅門經文（Sutra），因為在這個過程中，該詞的意義已經從簡短的助憶句子演變為充分展開的論述。可能原本經文（Sutra）是指在說法中待闡發的文本，因此冗長的佛教說法隨之被稱為經文（Sutta）、婆利舍婆提經文（Brihaspati-Sutra）。

從毗訶羅笈多經（Brihaspatif Sutras）的情況可以看出，某些較早期的哲學經文已經亡佚。據說這些經文曾包含徹頭徹尾的唯物主義者或感官主義者——樓迦耶陀派（Laukayatikas）或棣若瓦迦派（Tarvakas）——的學說，他們否定感官所能給予之物以外一切事物的存在。婆什迦羅笈多在《梵經》（Brahma-Sutras）III, 3, 53¹ 中曾引述過它們，由於他引錄了其中內容，這些經文在他那個時代很可能仍然存在，儘管至今在印度還未發現任何抄本。同樣的情況也適用於《毘克哈納薩經》（Vaikhanasa-Sutras）等經文，這些經文可能是為林棲期（Vanaprasthas）的修行者而作，以及《比丘經》（Bhiksu-Sutras）¹，帕尼尼在《語法書》IV, 3, 110 中曾引述過，似乎本意是為婆羅門教的而非佛教的乞食者而設。這是一個令人遺憾的事實，我們必須越來越認識到：在前佛教文獻中，我們僅保存了零散的片段，甚至在某些情況下，這些片段本身可能只是已亡佚的原典的複製本，如《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）的情況即是。我們現在知道，這類經文在任何時期都可能被撰作，我們也不應忘記，即使在當今梵文學術衰微之際，印度仍然有學者能夠模仿迦梨陀娑的風格，更勿論像《摩訶婆羅多》（Mahabharata）和《羅摩衍那》（Ramayana）這樣的詩作，而且模仿得如此成功，以至於少有歐洲梵文學者能夠看出區別。不久之前，我收到一部用經文體寫成且附有注疏的梵文論著，乃是印度一位在世學者的作品，足以欺騙許多歐洲梵文學者¹ ¹ 科爾布魯克《雜文集》2, I, 429。

• 3 塔拉那他‧塔爾卡瓦吉斯帕蒂（Taranatha Tarkavafcaspati）曾將其與吠檀多經論（Vedanta-Sutras）相提並論；見《妙諦寶鑑》（Siddhanta Kaunxudi）第一卷，第593頁。《參考文獻》。87 文獻 1. 如果這在現今是可能的，如同迦毘羅經論（Kapila-Sutras）在十四世紀時可能存在的情況，那麼為什麼同樣的事在印度的「文藝復興」時期，甚至更早的時代就不可能發生呢？無論如何，雖然我們應當感謝那些被保存下來的內容，以及以我們看來近乎奇蹟般的方式保存下來的內容，但我們不應該想像自己已經掌握了全部，或者已經掌握的內容仍保持其原始的形式。
參考書目
我在此僅提及某些最為重要的著作，使得哲學研究者，特別是那些不諳梵文者，能夠自行從中獲得對印度六大公認哲學體系的認識。較為重要的原始梵文文本標題可在寇爾布魯克（Colebrooke）的《雜集論文》第二卷第二三九頁及以後之處尋得，以及在他之後出版的歐洲與印度各地梵文手稿藏書的目錄中尋得。
關於跋陀羅衍那（Badaraya^a）的吠檀多哲學，最有用的著作是蒂博特（Thibaut）在《東方聖典叢書》第三十四及三十八卷中所譯的《經文》（Sutras）英文譯本及商羯羅（$amkara）的評註。
以德文撰寫的著作中，杜森（Deussen）於一八八七年所作的同一部著作之譯本，既以其一八八三年出版的《韋檀多系統》（*System des Vedânta*）為先導，實可強烈推薦。
關於數論（Sāṃkhya）體系，我們有巴蘭泰恩（Ballantyne）於一八八二至一八八五年所譯的經文（Sutras），以及《卡皮拉數論哲學的格言》（Aphorisms of the Sāṃkhya Philosophy of Kapila），附有來自註疏的說明性摘錄，版本分別出版於一八五二、一八六五和一八八五年。

在德文中，我們有《數論頌義疏》（Samkhya-Prava/cana-Bh&shya），即毗奢那笈多（Vigwana-bhikshu）對《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）的評註，由E·迦爾貝譯於1889年。此外還有阿尼魯陀（Aniruddha）的評註以及吠檀多學派大師摩訶提婆（Vedantin Mahadeva）對《數論經》的評註中的原始部分，均由理查德·迦爾貝譯出。¹ 它被稱為《迦坦多語法論釋》（Katantrafcfcftandafeprakriya），由讚德羅坎塔·塔卡蘭迦羅（Zandrakanta Tarkalankara）編纂於1896年，並為吠陀文法《迦坦多》（Katantra）增補了額外的經文。他毫不隱諱地表示「蘇特若-弗里蒂·斯歐跋雅木·阿匹·瑪雅伊瓦·ව్యරచ티」（Sutrara vnttis fcobhayam api mayaiva vyarafti），即「經文與評註，兩者皆由我所撰」。¹ 印度哲學
《商羯羅月光》(Der Mondschein der Samkhya Wahrheit)，即瓦亞帕蒂密希拉(Va/^aspatimisra)的《商羯羅哲理燈》(Samkhya-tattva-kaumudi)，由 R. 加貝(R. Garbe)譯於 1893 年，亦是一部極有用處的著作。
《數論頌》（Samkhya Karika）由弗·斯瓦拉克里希納撰述，從梵文譯成英文者為 H. T. 科爾布魯克，復有高若跋陀作《釋論》（Bhashya），經 H. H. 威爾遜翻譯並撰原注以資闡明，牛津版，1837 年，迄今仍堪參考。
其他有用的著作如下：——
約翰‧戴維斯，《印度哲學：伊瓦拉克里希那的數論頌》，倫敦，1881年。

《三數派哲學》，根據來源，由 K. 葛柏著。關於《前彌摩檫》（Purva-Mimamsa）或簡稱《彌摩檫》（Mimamsa），它主要論述吠陀的性質與權威性，並特別涉及祭祀與其他義務，我們已有《經文》（Sutras）及沙跋羅斯瓦敏（Sabarasvamin）的註疏之原文刊本；然而迄今尚無英文著作足以供人研習該體系，僅有提博教授（Professor Thibaut）對勞迦克希‧跋沙迦（Laugakshi Bhaskara）《義要集》（Arthasamgraha）的譯本可參，該書為該哲學的簡明摘要，刊載於《貝拿勒斯梵文叢書》（Benares Sanskrit Series）第四冊。

瓦伊舍希卡（Vaiseshika）哲學體系可以通過以下文獻進行研究：A. E. 高夫（A. E. Gough）的英文譯本《經文集》（Sutras），貝拿勒斯，一八七三年；以及羅爾（Roer）的德文譯本，載於《德國東方學會雜誌》（Zeitschrift der Deutschen Morgenlandischen Gesellschaft），第二十一卷及第二十二卷；以及本人在同一《德國東方學會期刊》（Journal of the German Oriental Society）中的若干文章，一八四九年。
歌德摩（Gotama）的《尼亞亞經》（Nyaya-Sutras）已由巴蘭泰恩（Ballantyne）翻譯成英文，惟最後一卷除外，出版地為阿拉哈巴德。《瑜伽經》（Yoga-Sutras）則可見拉堅德拉羅羅‧彌多羅（Rajendralala Mitra）的英文譯本，收錄於《印度文庫》（Bibliotheca Indica）第462、478、482、491及493號。

我注意到提供的英文原文「Bates of the Philosophical Sfttras.」存在以下問題： 1. "Bates" 可能是人名或印刷錯誤 2. "Sfttras" 應為 "Sutras"（經文/經典）的明顯印刷錯誤 3. 完整的語境不足以確定確切含義 根據穆勒《宗教學導論》（1873）的學術背景推測，此句可能應為： **《哲學經典的評述》** 或 **《哲學經文集》** 建議您確認原文是否完整或提供更多上下文，以便提供更準確的翻譯。

如果我們考察印度哲學思想的狀態，如同在《梵書》（Brahmawas）和《奧義書》（Upanishads）中所呈現給我們的情形，以及其後佛教的規範典籍中所表現的樣子，我們就不能奇怪，為什麼所有試圖確定六個公認哲學體系的年代的嘗試，乃至於它們彼此間的相互關係，迄今都未能成功。誠然，佛教和耆那教同樣只是眾多哲學體系中的兩個，且我們已經有可能確定它們的年代。但若論及它們的情形，我們對其年代和歷史發展有所瞭解，這主要乃是源於它們在西元前第五、第四和第三世紀期間所獲得的社會和政治重要性，而非單純源於它們的哲學信條。我們也知道，曾有許多教師是佛陀的同時代人，但他們在印度的文學史上未曾留下任何痕跡。

我們也不應忘記，儘管佛教聖典的年代可以確定，但我們現在所持有並接受為正統的許多文本的年代絕非毫無疑問。在佛教編年史本身中，其他教師如毘提訶笈多（?tf&tiputra）、尼乾陀、耆那教的創始人、布羅陀迦葉（PuraTia K&syapa）、迦羅迦栴延（Kakuda K&tyayana）、阿耆多翅舍欽婆羅（Ac/ita Kesakambali）、三彌提毘羅胝子（Sam#aya Vairaj^i-putra）、末羯利瞿舍羅（Gosali-putra）、末伽梨（the Maskarin）等，都與釋迦族的王子喬達摩並列被提及。其中只有一位在歷史上為人所知，即毘提訶笈多（?tf&tiputra）、尼乾陀或裸行苦行者，因為他所創立的教團，如同佛陀所創立的僧團一樣，發展成了一股強大的宗教勢力，即耆那教（ffainas）。另一位，持竹杖的末羯利瞿舍羅，原本是阿耆毘迦派信徒，後來成為大雄的追隨者，也成了自己宗派的創始人，但這個宗派現已消亡。毘提訶笈多或那陀笈多（Cr/iatiputra or N&taputta）實際上是佛陀的長者。
雖然六大哲學體系的創立者——儘管不是我們現存的經文（Sutras）的作者——似乎應屬於同一時期的哲學與宗教激盪，正是這一時期促成了佛陀教義在印度的最初傳播，但絕非明確可知這些體系中的任何一個在其文獻形式上是為佛教所預設的。這乃是由於引文的含混不清所造成，而此類引文幾乎從未被給出¹ ¹ Kern, Buddhismus, I, p. 182.

逐字逐句。在印度，於文獻記憶傳承時期，一部書的內容可能已有相當程度的修改，但書名卻保持不變。即便在較晚得多的時代，當我們看到跋陀羅笈多（卒於西元六五〇年）引述彌曼差派、數論派及勝論派的哲學思想（Darsanas）時，我們也無權斷定他對這些哲學思想的認識與我們現今的認識完全相同，儘管他很可能在這些哲學已形成系統的形式之後才認識它們。再者，當他引述正理派（Naiyayikas）時，決不能因此推斷他認識我們的《瞿曇經》（Gotama-Sutras），我們也無權聲稱我們的《瞿曇經》在他的時代已然存在。這是可能的，是合理的，但並非確定的。因此，我們必須格外謹慎，不應過度依賴對其他哲學體系的引述，或者更準確地說，是對它們的暗引。
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」似乎有誤或無法辨認： **"S&wkliy a- Sutras."** 這個文本無法確認為標準的英文。根據脈絡推測，這可能是： - 掃描或OCR錯誤 - 排版損壞的梵文術語 如果您是指 **"Sutras"**（經文），翻譯應為：**經文**或**蘇多羅**。 若您是指特定的吠陀經文集，請提供正確的英文原文，我將確保準確的繁體中文翻譯。

《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）如我們現存的版本所示，極少提及其他學派。當它們審視勝論派的六類（V, 85）和尼夜耶派的十六種範疇（Padarthas）（V, 86）時，明確地指涉了勝論派（Vaiseshika）和尼夜耶派（Nyaya）。每當它們提及原子（anu）時，我們知道它們心中想的是勝論派哲學；而且勝論派確實曾被提名一次（I, 35）。《吠陀》（Sruti）本應為數論派所貶低，但實際上被極為頻繁地援引，《傳承》（Smriti）被引用一次（V, 123），而瓦馬德瓦（Vamadeva）——其名同時出現於《吠陀》和《傳承》中——被提及為已獲得靈性解脫者。但在個別哲學家中，我們僅遇見桑底那‧阿陀耶（Sanndana Aditya）（VI, 69）及帕那夏卡（Panasikha）（V, 32; VI, 68），而當導師、阿闍黎（Acaryas）被籠統提及時，其所指乃包括迦毘羅本人及其他人物。
《韋檀多經》（Vedānta-Sūtras）中有更為頻繁的提及，但對於年代學的目的而言，它們也幫助不大。

毗耶娑（Bādarāyaṇa）多少都有提及佛教徒、耆那教徒（ffainas）、濕婆派（Pasupatas）及往世派（Patf&aratras），他試圖駁斥所有這些派系。然而，他從未引用任何吠檀多經（Vedanta-Sutras）的文獻著作；即便他提及其他哲學體系，他似乎也刻意迴避這些體系公認的學派名稱，甚至迴避其技術術語。儘管如此，明確的是，他在撰寫自己的經文（Sutras）時，心中存有前吠檀多派（PurvaMimamsa）、瑜伽派（Yoga）、數論派（Samkhya）及勝論派（Vaiseshika）等體系，而在彌曼差派權威中，他逐名提及柴摩尼（ffaimini）、跋達里（Badari）、歐迦摩尼（AucMomi）、阿斯瑪剌迦亞（Asmarathya）、迦娑克里哲那（Kasakritsna）、迦勒須那古尼（Karshnaguni）及阿特雷耶（Atreya），甚至還提及跋達剌耶那（BadarayaTia）。因此，若將六大哲學體系的漸進形成歸屬於佛陀時代（西元前五世紀）至阿育王時代（西元前三世紀）之間的時期，我們不會有太大的偏差；儘管我們必須承認，特別是在吠檀多派、數論派及瑜伽派的情況下，存有一段長期的先前發展，其源遠可及奧義書（Upanishads）與梵書（Br&hma72,as），乃至梨俱吠陀（Big-veda）本身的讚歌。

要確定偉大的哲學體系之間的相對位置同樣困難，因為如我之前所解釋的，它們相互引用彼此的著作。關於佛教與六大正統體系之間的關係，似乎我們所能誠實地說的是，佛教經典（Buddhist Suttas）預設了傳承與我們六大古典或正統體系非常相似之教義的哲學學派。但這與某些學者所持的意見大相逕庭，彼等認為佛陀或其弟子實際上曾向我們的經典（Sutras）借取思想。在《數論頌》（Sāṃkhya-kārikas）之前，我們對數論文獻一無所知，而《數論頌》屬於基督紀元後第六世紀。即使我們承認《本質撮要》（Tattva-samasa）是更早期的著作，在沒有相應年代對照的情況下，我們如何能證明佛陀或其弟子在那麼早的時期確實有過任何借取之舉呢？
在《奧義書》與《梵書》（Brāhmaṇas）中，儘管貫穿其間有某種共同的主旋律，但仍然存在著很大的自由度與缺乏體系之處，以及由不同的導師與不同的學派所支持的各種觀點。即便在讚頌詩中，我們也遇見了思想上的極大獨立性與個性，這種獨立性與個性有時似乎達到了直言不諱的懷疑主義與無神論的程度。
班達卡（Bhandarkar），《數論哲學》（Sawkhya Philosophy），一八七一年，第三頁。
我們必須牢記所有這些要點，才能正確理解我們習稱的印度六大哲學體系的歷史起源與發展。我們已經看到，哲學討論並非婆羅門所獨佔，剎帝利也在基本哲學概念（如阿特曼〔Atman〕或自我）的闡發中發揮了非常積極且顯著的角色。
在印度作為共同財產流傳著的這種龐雜的哲學與宗教觀點的基礎之上，規範化的體系才逐漸萌生。雖然我們不知道這個過程以何種形式發生，但有一點是十分明確的：我們現今所掌有的哲學手冊，其形式為《蘇特羅》(Sutras)，不可能是在印度尚不知曉書寫、或至少除了在紀念碑與錢幣的銘刻之外還未將書寫應用於文學用途的時代所撰寫的——至少據我們所知是如此。
